threats.title: claim() collect(type(uint128).max) Steals Other Investors' Fees and Permanently DoS Subsequent Claims
threats.hypothesis_description: ILOPool.claim() uses pool.collect(type(uint128).max) which sweeps ALL tokens owed to the shared Uniswap V3 position (all investors share one position key). The first claimer's call collects all accumulated trading fees for all investors. The user receives their calculated fee share, and the remainder (other investors' fees) is sent to FEE_TAKER. When subsequent investors call claim(), their fee calculation uses feeGrowthDelta * positionLiquidity, producing a non-zero expected amount. But the tokens backing those fees were already collected and sent to FEE_TAKER. The subtraction amountCollected0 - amount0 underflows in Solidity 0.7.6 (unchecked arithmetic), causing the safeTransfer to attempt sending a massive amount, which reverts. All subsequent claimers are permanently locked out.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a fundamental accounting flaw in the shared-position fee distribution model. Any claim after the first one that expects historical fees will revert, permanently locking investor funds.

proof_of_signal.pattern: pool.collect(address(this), TICK_LOWER, TICK_UPPER, type(uint128).max, type(uint128).max) collects ALL owed tokens for the shared position, then amountCollected0-amount0 sent to feeTaker includes other users' uncollected fees

preconditions: Pool has been launched successfully (_launchSucceeded == true)
Trading has occurred on the Uniswap V3 pool generating fees
At least two investors hold positions in the ILOPool
First investor's vesting schedule has unlocked some liquidity

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: collect(type(uint128).max) sweeps all position fees, not just the claiming user's proportional share
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: No admin action required - any investor claiming triggers the issue
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent from the moment any trading fees accumulate post-launch

operational_questions: Does burn() settle fees for the entire position or only the burned portion?
After A's collect(max), what is the actual tokensOwed when B burns their portion?
Can the underflow in amountCollected0 - amount0 ever be avoided?
validation_requirements: Deploy ILOPool with 2+ investors, generate trading fees, have investor A claim, then verify investor B's claim reverts
Verify that feeGrowthInside values reflect the full history for unclaimed users
Quantify the exact fee loss per non-first claimer

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Wait for ILO pool launch and trading fee accumulation
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Any investor (or FEE_TAKER acting strategically) claims their vested position first
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: collect(max) drains all accumulated fees from the shared Uniswap position
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: All subsequent investors' claim() calls revert due to underflow, permanently locking their funds
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None - any normal claim triggers this. Being the first claimer is advantageous.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: All investors except the first claimer permanently lose access to their vested liquidity and fees
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: No on-chain recovery possible - funds permanently locked in Uniswap V3 position

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Low - single transaction by any position holder
execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Basic - just call claim() on a vested position
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: After launch and any fee accumulation

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: First claim transaction followed by reverted claim attempts
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Large fee transfers to FEE_TAKER not proportional to single user's share
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: No abnormal behavior needed - normal usage triggers this

threats.title: Whitelist Bypass: Non-Whitelisted Addresses Pass checkWhitelist Due to Default Mapping Value Logic Flaw
threats.hypothesis_description: In Whitelist.checkWhitelist(), the condition `if (_allowedWhitelistIndex == 0 || _whitelistIndex[to] > _allowedWhitelistIndex)` is intended to block non-whitelisted buyers. However, non-whitelisted addresses have _whitelistIndex[to] == 0 (Solidity mapping default). When _allowedWhitelistIndex > 0, the first condition is false, and `0 > _allowedWhitelistIndex` is also false (0 is never greater than a positive integer). Neither condition triggers the revert, so non-whitelisted addresses freely pass the check. The whitelist mechanism is completely ineffective.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a logic error in the core whitelist enforcement. The check should verify that the user HAS a whitelist index (non-zero) AND that index is within the allowed range.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (_allowedWhitelistIndex == 0 || _whitelistIndex[to] > _allowedWhitelistIndex) revert; default mapping value 0 bypasses the > check

preconditions: _allowedWhitelistIndex > 0 (whitelist is active, some users allowed)
_locked == false (trading not fully locked)
Buyer address is not blacklisted
Transfer originates from _pool (Uniswap V3 buy)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Default Solidity mapping value (0) not treated as 'unwhitelisted' in the comparison logic
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None - exploitable by any address once whitelist is active

operational_questions: Can non-whitelisted addresses buy unlimited VULT tokens from the pool?
Does the maxAddressCap still apply (limiting damage per address)?
How many non-whitelisted addresses could exploit this simultaneously?
validation_requirements: Call checkWhitelist with a never-whitelisted address when _allowedWhitelistIndex > 0
Verify the function does not revert
Confirm buy from pool succeeds for non-whitelisted address

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Wait for _allowedWhitelistIndex to be set > 0 and _locked to be set false
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Buy VULT from Uniswap V3 pool from any non-whitelisted address
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: _beforeTokenTransfer calls checkWhitelist, which passes despite no whitelist entry
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Repeat from multiple addresses to bypass maxAddressCap per address
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None - single attacker with multiple addresses

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Token distribution fairness destroyed; whales can accumulate unlimited tokens
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Cannot be fixed without contract redeployment or blacklisting exploiters
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All buy actions from Uniswap V3 pool during whitelist period

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Any time during active whitelist period

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Token accumulation by addresses not in whitelist
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Any buy from pool by non-whitelisted address

threats.title: Per-User Cap Bypass via NFT Transfer Enables Single User to Monopolize ILO Allocation
threats.hypothesis_description: ILOPool.buy() checks `balanceOf(recipient) == 0` to determine if a new NFT should be minted. The maxCapPerUser is enforced against the current position's raiseAmount. If a user transfers their NFT to another wallet, balanceOf drops to 0, and a new position with raiseAmount=0 is created on the next buy. The per-user cap resets. This can be repeated to accumulate up to hardCap worth of RAISE_TOKEN across multiple wallets, all controlled by one entity.
threats.investigation_rationale: The cap enforcement is per-NFT-position, not per-historical-address. ERC721 transferability creates a trivial bypass where transferring the NFT resets the user's apparent contribution to zero.

proof_of_signal.pattern: balanceOf(recipient) == 0 triggers new NFT mint; per-user cap checked against _position.raiseAmount of current NFT only; NFT is transferable ERC721

preconditions: User is whitelisted (or _openToAll is true)
Sale is active (between start and end time)
hardCap has not been reached
User controls at least 2 addresses

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: ERC721 transferFrom resets balanceOf to 0, triggering fresh NFT mint and clean raiseAmount on next buy()

operational_questions: Can transferring NFT and re-buying be done atomically via multicall?
Does the whitelist check apply to the new address receiving the NFT?
How quickly can an attacker exhaust the hardCap?
validation_requirements: Buy maxCapPerUser, transfer NFT to second wallet, buy again from original wallet
Verify new NFT minted with raiseAmount=0 and cap check passes
Confirm total raised across positions exceeds maxCapPerUser for the original address

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Buy maxCapPerUser from whitelisted address A
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Transfer NFT from A to secondary address B
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Buy maxCapPerUser again from address A (new NFT minted, cap reset)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Repeat until hardCap reached, accumulating all allocation

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: ILO allocation monopolized by single entity, destroying fair distribution
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Cannot be reversed - purchases are final once made
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Fair distribution guarantee of ILO sale

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Low - one attacker with multiple wallets

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Multiple NFT mints to same address with intermediate transfers
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Single address exceeding maxCapPerUser across multiple positions
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: NFT transfers immediately followed by new buy() calls

threats.title: Missing Upper Bound Price Range Validation in initILOPool Allows Misconfigured Pools That Trap Investor Funds
threats.hypothesis_description: ILOManager.initILOPool validates `sqrtRatioLowerX96 < _project.initialPoolPriceX96 && sqrtRatioLowerX96 < sqrtRatioUpperX96` but does NOT verify `_project.initialPoolPriceX96 < sqrtRatioUpperX96`. If the initial price is at or above the upper tick, the concentrated liquidity math in buy() and launch() produces incorrect results. Specifically, _saleAmountNeeded and liquidity calculations will use inverted parameters in LiquidityAmounts, creating positions where the raise-to-liquidity conversion is fundamentally wrong. Since initProject is permissionless, anyone can create a malicious project with this misconfiguration.
threats.investigation_rationale: The RANGE check is incomplete - only one inequality of a required two-sided bound is enforced. This allows creation of ILO pools where the price/tick relationship violates Uniswap V3 concentrated liquidity assumptions.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(sqrtRatioLowerX96 < _project.initialPoolPriceX96 && sqrtRatioLowerX96 < sqrtRatioUpperX96, 'RANGE') missing check that initialPoolPriceX96 < sqrtRatioUpperX96

preconditions: Attacker calls initProject to become project admin
Sets tickUpper such that sqrtRatioUpperX96 <= initialPoolPriceX96
Creates ILO pool with these parameters

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Only lower < initial checked, not initial < upper; LiquidityAmounts.getLiquidityForAmount0/1 auto-swap parameters when A > B, masking the error
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Exploitable by any project admin (permissionless to become one)

operational_questions: What exact liquidity values does _saleAmountNeeded return when price >= upper tick?
Would launch() succeed or fail with miscalculated amounts?
Can investors' RAISE_TOKEN be permanently locked in a pool that can never launch?
validation_requirements: Create pool with initialPoolPriceX96 >= sqrtRatioUpperX96
Execute buy() and verify liquidity calculation correctness
Attempt launch() and verify behavior

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Call initProject with chosen token pair, becoming project admin
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call initILOPool with tickUpper yielding sqrtRatioUpperX96 <= initialPoolPriceX96
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Investors buy into the misconfigured pool, receiving incorrect liquidity amounts
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Launch fails or produces incorrect Uniswap position; investor funds trapped until refund deadline

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Investor funds locked in a non-launchable pool until refund deadline (7+ days)
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Must wait for refund deadline; funds at market risk during lock period
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: buy(), launch() for the affected pool

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Moderate - understanding of Uniswap V3 tick/price math

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Pool creation with abnormal tick ranges
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Project creation followed by rapid investor deposits

threats.title: buy() Griefing: Anyone Can Fill a Whitelisted User's Allocation Without Their Consent
threats.hypothesis_description: ILOPool.buy(raiseAmount, recipient) accepts an arbitrary recipient parameter and checks whitelist against recipient, not msg.sender. The RAISE_TOKEN is pulled from msg.sender, but the position is created/updated for recipient. An attacker can call buy(maxCapPerUser, victimAddress) with their own RAISE_TOKEN, filling the victim's allocation cap. The victim can no longer participate in the ILO because their per-user cap is exhausted. The attacker loses their RAISE_TOKEN (it goes into the pool for the victim's position), but the victim is denied participation at their desired timing or amount.
threats.investigation_rationale: The separation of payer (msg.sender) and recipient allows unsolicited allocation-filling. While the attacker loses funds, the griefing impact on targeted whitelisted users is significant.

proof_of_signal.pattern: buy(raiseAmount, recipient) - whitelist checked on recipient but RAISE_TOKEN pulled from msg.sender; no check that msg.sender == recipient or has recipient's authorization

preconditions: Victim address is whitelisted
Sale is active
Attacker has sufficient RAISE_TOKEN and has approved ILOPool

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: No consent mechanism: msg.sender can buy on behalf of any whitelisted recipient

operational_questions: Can the victim transfer the unwanted NFT and rebuy (combining with OPS-H003)?
What is the attacker's financial loss vs the griefing impact?
Can this be used to block high-value whitelisted participants?
validation_requirements: Call buy(maxCapPerUser, victimAddress) from attacker address
Verify victim's cap is exhausted
Verify victim cannot buy additional allocation

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Approve ILOPool to spend attacker's RAISE_TOKEN
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Call buy(maxCapPerUser, victimAddress) for each target
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Victims cannot participate at their desired allocation

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Targeted whitelisted users denied ILO participation
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Victims could use NFT transfer workaround (OPS-H003)

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Positions created for users who didn't initiate the buy
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Rapid filling of multiple users' caps by single address

threats.title: Liquidity Accounting Mismatch Between Per-Buy and Aggregate Launch Calculations Locks Late Claimers' Funds
threats.hypothesis_description: In buy(), each investor's liquidity is calculated individually: getLiquidity(raiseAmount_i) * shares / BPS with floor division at each step. In launch(), total liquidity is calculated from totalRaised: getLiquidity(totalRaised). Due to non-linearity of getLiquidity functions and accumulated floor-division rounding, sum(individual_liquidities) may exceed the actual Uniswap position liquidity allocated to investors (liquidity * investorShares / BPS). If the sum exceeds reality, late claimers' burn() calls will revert because the Uniswap position has insufficient liquidity.
threats.investigation_rationale: getLiquidityForAmount0/1 is not strictly linear with respect to amount. Multiple small inputs produce different aggregate liquidity than one large input. With many investors, cumulative rounding error can become material.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Per-user: getLiquidityForAmount0/1(raiseAmount) then mulDiv(liquidityDelta, shares, BPS) with floor division; launch: _saleAmountNeeded(totalRaised) produces different total liquidity

preconditions: Multiple investors have purchased positions via buy()
Pool has launched successfully
All investors attempt to claim their full vested liquidity

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Individual liquidity calculations use floor division per call; aggregate calculation at launch uses a single floor division
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: After all vesting unlocks, when total claims exceed available liquidity

operational_questions: What is the maximum per-investor rounding error?
With N investors, does cumulative error exceed Uniswap position dust?
Can the last claimer's burn() revert due to insufficient liquidity?
validation_requirements: Simulate 100+ buy calls with varying amounts
Compare sum of individual liquidities to total launch liquidity
Attempt full claim of all positions sequentially

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Not an active exploit - occurs naturally with multiple investors
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: All investors claim their full positions as vesting unlocks
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Last investor(s) receive insufficient liquidity from Uniswap burn
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Their claim() reverts, permanently locking their remaining position

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Last claimer(s) cannot withdraw vested funds
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: No on-chain recovery for the rounding-dust locked liquidity
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: claim() for the last position(s) to claim

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Reverted claim transactions for the last remaining positions
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Sum of tracked liquidities > actual Uniswap position liquidity

threats.title: Zero-Duration Vesting Schedule Permanently DoS Claims via Division by Zero in _unlockedLiquidity
threats.hypothesis_description: ILOVest._validateVestSchedule does not check that schedule[i].start < schedule[i].end. If start == end (zero-duration vest), _unlockedLiquidity hits the else branch when block.timestamp >= start && block.timestamp == end (or rather, vest.end < block.timestamp won't be true when block.timestamp == vest.end == vest.start). In the else branch: (vest.end - vest.start) * BPS = 0, causing FullMath.mulDiv to revert on zero denominator. Since claim() calls _claimableLiquidity which calls _unlockedLiquidity, all claims for positions with zero-duration vests permanently revert.
threats.investigation_rationale: Missing validation of vest.start < vest.end allows construction of unclaimed-forever positions. Since initProject is permissionless, any attacker can become project admin and configure malicious vesting.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _validateVestSchedule checks schedule[i].start >= lastEnd but never checks start < end; _unlockedLiquidity line: (vest.end - vest.start) * BPS used as denominator

preconditions: Malicious project admin creates ILO pool with vesting schedule where start == end
Investors buy into the pool (they see vesting config but may not detect the issue)
Pool launches successfully

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: _validateVestSchedule allows start == end; _unlockedLiquidity divides by (end - start) which is 0
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires project admin to configure malicious vesting (anyone can become project admin via permissionless initProject)
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: At pool initialization; permanent effect after launch

operational_questions: Does _validateVestSchedule accept start == end?
Does _unlockedLiquidity always hit the else branch for zero-duration vests?
Can investors detect this before buying?
validation_requirements: Create pool with vest schedule entry where start == end
Confirm _validateVestSchedule passes
Attempt claim after launch and verify division by zero revert

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Call initProject to become admin, then initILOPool with zero-duration vest schedule
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Wait for investors to buy into the pool
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: All investor claims permanently revert due to division by zero

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: All investor positions permanently locked with no claim path
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: No on-chain recovery - funds trapped in Uniswap position forever
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: claim() for all investors in the affected pool

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Vesting configs with identical start and end timestamps
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: All claim attempts reverting for a specific pool
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Project admin who creates pool but never participates

threats.title: Oracle TWAP Fallback to Minimal Period on New Pools Enables Whitelist Cap Bypass via Price Manipulation
threats.hypothesis_description: UniswapV3Oracle.peek() uses `period = PERIOD < longestPeriod ? PERIOD : longestPeriod`. For a newly created or recently initialized pool, getOldestObservationSecondsAgo returns a very small value (seconds). The TWAP window shrinks to near-spot price, making it trivially manipulable. An attacker can manipulate the pool price to make VULT appear extremely cheap in ETH terms, causing the oracle to report a low ETH equivalent. Whitelist.checkWhitelist then allows purchases far exceeding the intended maxAddressCap since the estimated ETH value is artificially depressed.
threats.investigation_rationale: The oracle has no minimum safe period check. On a new pool, the TWAP degenerates to spot price, which can be manipulated via large swaps or flash loans in a multi-block strategy.

proof_of_signal.pattern: uint32 period = PERIOD < longestPeriod ? PERIOD : longestPeriod; no minimum period enforcement; newly created pool has longestPeriod near 0

preconditions: VULT/WETH Uniswap V3 pool has insufficient observation history (< 30 minutes)
Whitelist is active (_allowedWhitelistIndex > 0, _locked == false)
Attacker has sufficient capital to move the pool price

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: TWAP falls back to shortest available period, degenerating to spot price
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None - depends on pool's observation history
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: First ~30 minutes after pool creation or observation reset

operational_questions: What is the actual observation history when oracle is first configured?
How much capital is needed to move VULT/WETH price by 10x for one block?
Does the 5% slippage adjustment in peek() provide meaningful protection?
validation_requirements: Check getOldestObservationSecondsAgo for the VULT/WETH pool at deployment
Simulate price manipulation cost vs whitelist cap bypass benefit
Verify oracle returns manipulated price with short observation window

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Wait for or identify period when pool observation history is short
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Execute large swap to depress VULT price in ETH terms
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Buy VULT from pool - oracle reports low ETH equivalent, bypassing maxAddressCap
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Reverse the price manipulation in subsequent block
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Single attacker with sufficient capital for temporary price manipulation

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Per-address contribution cap bypassed, unfair token accumulation
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Tokens already purchased cannot be reclaimed

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Shortly after pool creation when observation history is minimal

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Large swaps immediately before VULT purchases exceeding expected caps
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Oracle returning values significantly below market price
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Rapid large purchases during pool's first minutes

threats.title: Reentrancy in launch() via SALE_TOKEN Callback Before _launchSucceeded Flag Set
threats.hypothesis_description: In ILOPool.launch(), _launchSucceeded is set to true as the LAST statement, after _refundProject(admin) which calls TransferHelper.safeTransfer(SALE_TOKEN, projectAdmin, refundAmount). If SALE_TOKEN is an ERC777 or token with transfer hooks, the callback can re-enter the ILOPool. Since _launchSucceeded is still false, the refundable() modifier could pass (if block.timestamp >= refundDeadline), setting _refundTriggered = true. This corrupts the state machine: both _launchSucceeded and _refundTriggered become true, violating the mutual exclusivity invariant.
threats.investigation_rationale: The checks-effects-interactions pattern is violated: the critical state flag _launchSucceeded is set after external calls. With non-standard ERC20 tokens (which the documentation states are expected), reentrancy is plausible.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _refundProject(_project.admin) [external SALE_TOKEN transfer] executes BEFORE _launchSucceeded = true on line 246

preconditions: SALE_TOKEN has transfer hooks (ERC777, custom callback)
block.timestamp >= refundDeadline at launch time (possible if owner manipulated deadline)
Reentrancy path through claimRefund or claimProjectRefund

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: _launchSucceeded set after _refundProject external call; CEI pattern violated
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: May require ILOManager owner to set favorable refundDeadline
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: During launch() execution when SALE_TOKEN transfer triggers callback

operational_questions: Can claimRefund succeed during reentrancy (is RAISE_TOKEN still in contract)?
What state corruption occurs if both flags are set true?
Would the RAISE_TOKEN transfer in claimRefund fail (already sent to Uniswap)?
validation_requirements: Test with ERC777 SALE_TOKEN that calls back during launch()
Verify refundable() modifier passes during callback
Check if both flags can be set simultaneously

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Deploy project with ERC777 or hook-enabled SALE_TOKEN
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Trigger launch() - during _refundProject SALE_TOKEN transfer callback, call claimRefund
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires control of SALE_TOKEN contract (project admin)

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Unpredictable behavior with both lifecycle flags true
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: No recovery mechanism - state corruption permanent

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: launch() and claimRefund events in same transaction

threats.title: ILOManager Constructor Uses tx.origin for Ownership Creating Deployment Security Gap
threats.hypothesis_description: ILOManager constructor calls `transferOwnership(tx.origin)`. When deployed via a factory, Create2 deployer, multisig, or deployment pipeline, tx.origin is the EOA that initiated the outer transaction, not necessarily the intended owner. Between deployment and initialize() (which transfers ownership to initialOwner), the tx.origin address has full owner privileges and can call setPlatformFee, setPerformanceFee, setFeeTaker, setILOPoolImplementation, etc. The deployment script (Deploy.s.sol) deploys and initializes in the same broadcast, but if initialize() fails or is called separately, the window exists.
threats.investigation_rationale: tx.origin-based ownership is a known anti-pattern. The deployment script partially mitigates by initializing in the same transaction, but separate deployment/initialization (Init.s.sol exists as separate script) creates a real window.
deployment_assumptions: ILOManager deployment and initialization happen in separate transactions
threats.config_evidence: Init.s.sol exists as a separate initialization script, indicating separate deployment/init is a supported workflow

proof_of_signal.pattern: constructor () { transferOwnership(tx.origin); } - tx.origin used instead of msg.sender

preconditions: ILOManager deployed via factory/multisig where tx.origin != intended owner
Gap between deployment and initialize() call
tx.origin address is aware of ownership or malicious

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: tx.origin ownership in constructor + separate Init.s.sol deployment script

operational_questions: Is ILOManager always deployed and initialized atomically?
Can the tx.origin owner call setILOPoolImplementation before initialize()?
Does the Initializable modifier protect against owner abuse before initialization?
validation_requirements: Verify deployment script atomicity
Check if owner functions work before initialize() (afterInitialize guard on some functions)
Test tx.origin ownership with factory deployment

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Monitor for ILOManager deployment without immediate initialization
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: tx.origin (if compromised) calls setILOPoolImplementation with malicious implementation
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: All future ILO pools cloned from malicious implementation
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: initialize() called later, transfers ownership away, but damage is done

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Temporary full admin access to ILOManager
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Malicious pool implementation affecting all future pools
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Owner can fix after initialization, but early pools may be compromised

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Owner function calls between deployment and initialization
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Multiple ownership transfers in rapid succession

threats.title: ILOManager Owner Can Set Refund Deadline to Far Future Permanently Locking Investor Funds
threats.hypothesis_description: ILOManager.setRefundDeadlineForProject has no constraints - the owner can set refundDeadline to type(uint64).max. Combined with a scenario where launch() fails (e.g., Uniswap V3 pool price changed), both the launch path (price mismatch revert) and the refund path (deadline never reached) become permanently blocked. All investor RAISE_TOKEN and project SALE_TOKEN are permanently locked in ILO pools with no recovery mechanism.
threats.investigation_rationale: The refund deadline is the only fallback mechanism for failed sales. If it can be set to an unreachable value, the safety net is removed entirely.

proof_of_signal.pattern: setRefundDeadlineForProject has no bounds check; can set to type(uint64).max making refundable() modifier's block.timestamp >= refundDeadline unreachable

preconditions: ILOManager owner is malicious or compromised
Project has active ILO pools with investor deposits
Launch cannot proceed (price manipulation or other failure)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: No upper bound on refundDeadline parameter
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time before launch or refund is triggered

operational_questions: Is ILOManager owner a multisig or timelock?
Can refundDeadline be changed after _refundTriggered is set?
Is there any emergency mechanism to bypass this?
validation_requirements: Call setRefundDeadlineForProject with type(uint64).max
Verify claimRefund reverts with 'RFT'
Confirm no alternative withdrawal path exists

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: ILOManager owner sets refundDeadline to type(uint64).max for target project
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Manipulate Uniswap V3 pool price to prevent launch()
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Both launch and refund paths are permanently blocked
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: All investor RAISE_TOKEN locked with no recovery

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Complete permanent loss of all investor funds in affected pools
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: No on-chain recovery possible - requires contract migration or hard fork

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Before launch or refund is triggered

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Refund deadline set far in the future
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Owner modifying deadlines for active projects

threats.title: Missing Fee Upper Bound Validation Allows Owner to DoS All Claims via Underflow in _deductFees
threats.hypothesis_description: ILOManager.setPlatformFee and setPerformanceFee accept any uint16 value (up to 65535) with no upper bound check. If PLATFORM_FEE is set >= 10000 BPS (100%), _deductFees computes: amount0Left = amount0 - FullMath.mulDiv(amount0, feeBPS, 10000). When feeBPS >= 10000, the subtracted value >= amount0, causing underflow in Solidity 0.7.6 (no overflow protection). This produces a massive erroneous amount that fails on transfer, permanently DOSing all claim() calls. Fees are snapshot at project creation time in _cachedProject, so this affects all pools created after the fee change.
threats.investigation_rationale: uint16 can hold values up to 65535 but BPS should be < 10000. No validation exists, and the fee is applied in unchecked arithmetic.

proof_of_signal.pattern: setPlatformFee(uint16 _platformFee) has no require(_platformFee < BPS); _deductFees subtracts fee amount from total with no overflow check in Solidity 0.7.6

preconditions: ILOManager owner sets PLATFORM_FEE or PERFORMANCE_FEE >= 10000
New project is created after fee change (fees snapshot at creation)
Pool launches and investors attempt to claim

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: No bounds check on fee setter; Solidity 0.7.6 unchecked arithmetic in _deductFees
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires ILOManager owner to set excessive fees
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: After fee change, for all new projects

operational_questions: Can fees be changed retroactively for existing projects?
Does the uint16 type limit provide any practical protection?
What is the exact revert behavior in _deductFees with fee >= 10000?
validation_requirements: Set PLATFORM_FEE to 10001, create project, launch pool, attempt claim
Verify _deductFees underflows
Confirm claim() permanently reverts

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: New project created with fee=10001 snapshot
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: All claim() calls revert due to underflow in _deductFees

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: All investor claims permanently blocked for affected projects
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: No recovery mechanism - funds locked in Uniswap position
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: claim() for all pools created after fee change

investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Fee changes followed by new project creation
